advaita.rendering: Whoever desires to worship whatever form of deity with faith — that very faith I, the inner controller (antaryāmin), make firm and unwavering in him. Śaṅkara reads this as the Lord stabilizing the natural inclination (svabhāva-pravṛtti) of the desire-driven worshiper toward his chosen form. The verse does not praise such worship; it explains the mechanism by which Īśvara upholds even secondary devotion, since all forms are ultimately non-different from the one Brahman.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'yasyaiva pūrvam pravṛttaḥ svabhāvato yaḥ yāṃ devatātanuṃ śraddhayā arcitum icchati' — the faith is steadied, not newly created; the direction was already set by prior nature.
advaita.divergence_note: Advaita does not celebrate the worshiper of limited forms; it explains why the Absolute sustains even limited worship without contradiction.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Even those who unknowingly (ajānat) worship Indra or Āditya are worshiping the Lord's own body-forms (madīyāḥ tanavas), for the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad declares 'He who dwells in the sun, whom the sun does not know — his body is the sun.' Knowing this, I Myself trace (anusandhāya) that faith as directed to My own bodily manifestation and make it firm, free from obstruction (nirvighna). The devotee's ignorance of the identity does not diminish the efficacy of the śraddhā, because the Lord Himself rectifies the address.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja: 'ajānann api matto anusandhāya tām evācalāṃ nirvighnaṃ vidadhāmi' — the Lord's active inner witnessing converts misdirected faith into effective devotion.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Unlike Advaita, Rāmānuja insists the individual devotee and the form worshiped are real; Bhagavān does not dissolve them but inhabits them as body-soul (śarīra-ātma-bhāva).

dvaita.rendering: Whatever form — from Brahmā downward (brahmādirūpa) — the devotee wishes to worship, that form is ultimately dependent upon Hari; and it is Hari who makes the śraddhā stable. The Nāradīya scripture confirms that devotees of Brahmā are counted among Bhagavān's own devotees (mad-bhaktānām anantatā), establishing a hierarchy: secondary deities are real but wholly subordinate. Worship of them is efficacious only insofar as Viṣṇu sustains it from within.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva cites Nāradīya: 'anto brahmādibhaktānāṃ mad-bhaktānām anantatā' — even Brahmā-worshipers are ultimately within the circle of Hari-devotion.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva's realism is uncompromising: the jīva, the deity, and Hari are three genuinely distinct reals; Hari's sustaining role does not collapse them.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: All deity-forms are Kṛṣṇa's own body-forms (mac-charīrabhūta); the worshiper who does not know this nevertheless worships Me. From within (antaḥ), recognizing that this faith is directed at My own bodily manifestation, I alone (aham eka) trace it and make it firm. In Vallabha's reading, even the unconscious convergence of śraddhā toward any form is an event within Kṛṣṇa's own līlā — the devotee's not-knowing does not place him outside the Lord's prasāda.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'mac-charīrabhūtaviṣayā eṣā śraddhā ity aham eko anusandhāya tāṃ śraddhām evācalāṃ vidadhāmi' — the singular pronoun 'aham eka' signals Puṣṭi-mārga's exclusive dependence on Kṛṣṇa's grace.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Śuddhādvaita reads no ontological gap between Kṛṣṇa and the world of forms; all forms are real and all are His body, so no worship truly misses Him.

bhakti.rendering: Among all devotees, whoever wishes to worship whatever deity-form — which is actually My own mūrti (madīyaṃ eva mūrtim) — I, as the indwelling controller (antaryāmin), make that very faith toward that form firm and steady. Śrīdhara reads 'tām eva' (that very faith, not a substitute) as the key: the Lord does not redirect the worshiper but deepens the faith already present, because every deity-form is already His.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'tad-tan-mūrti-viṣayāṃ tām eva śraddhām acalāṃ dṛḍhām aham antaryāmī vidadhāmi karomi' — the antaryāmin does not override but consolidates.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara's philological balance avoids both the impersonalism of Advaita and the exclusive hierarchism of Dvaita; all forms are real mūrtis of the one Lord.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: There is no danger that devotees of secondary deities will remain distant from Bhagavān Vāsudeva, because I Myself, as the inner controller, stabilize the faith that arises from the force of prior-life tendencies (janmāntara-vāsanā-bala-prādurbhūtā). Madhusūdana emphasizes that this stabilizing faith is directed toward the chosen deity-form — not yet toward Me directly — and acknowledges that limitation honestly: 'I make that very faith firm, not faith directed at Me.' Yet precisely this honesty about the worshiper's current level is the ground of compassion that will eventually draw him higher.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'tasyāḥ eva devatātanuṃ prati śraddhāṃ... acalāṃ sthirāṃ vidadhāmi... na tu mad-viṣayāṃ śraddhāṃ tasya tasya karomi ity arthaḥ' — a rare explicit admission of the verse's limiting clause.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana alone among the six explicitly names the limitation: the faith stabilized here is not yet bhakti toward Kṛṣṇa; it is a preparatory grace that the synthesis-path will eventually complete.

so_what_questions: If the Lord stabilizes faith in any form, does that imply all objects of worship are equally efficacious — or is there a difference in what the worshiper eventually receives (BG 7.22–23 addresses this)?
Madhusūdana explicitly says the Lord does NOT redirect the worshiper's faith toward Himself here — what does this reveal about how gradual spiritual development actually works?
Rāmānuja's claim that ignorant worship is still valid because all forms are the Lord's body: does this make sincere wrong-belief as effective as informed devotion?

Śaṅkara treats this verse as explanatory (why the world runs) rather than prescriptive (how to worship) — does the difference between descriptive and normative readings of Gītā verses change how we should use them?

Madhva's hierarchy — Hari sustains all, even Brahmā-worshipers are 'within' His circle — raises the question: is there any sincere act of worship that falls entirely outside the Lord's sustaining will?
The word 'acalā' (unwavering) appears in all six readings: is firm faith in a limited object a spiritual asset or a spiritual obstacle, and does the answer differ by school?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself drawn repeatedly toward a particular form of practice — a specific teacher, a ritual, a name — do not force a philosophical override. Recognize that inclination as your current svabhāva (natural disposition), use it fully, and let the practice itself reveal the formless ground beneath it.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Serve whatever role or relationship you are genuinely drawn to — parent, teacher, colleague, community — as if the Lord inhabits that form as its inner soul. The sincerity of service matters more than the theological precision of the address; Bhagavān traces the intention inward.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In a team or institution, acknowledge the real authority above you without collapsing that hierarchy into vague unity. Your manager, your mentor, the tradition you belong to — these are real, distinct, and dependent on a higher order; honoring the chain is itself a form of worship.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Stop waiting for the 'right' practice before beginning. Whatever form of beauty, devotion, or creative work genuinely moves you — enter it fully, knowing that Kṛṣṇa's grace is already present in that pull. The longing itself is His prasāda.
everyday_applications.bhakti: When you notice a genuine enthusiasm or faith beginning to form — toward a person, a cause, a practice — do not immediately intellectualize it away. The antaryāmin is already at work deepening it; your task is not to redirect it but to stay honest and attentive within it.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Recognize the stage you are actually at, not the stage you wish you were at. Madhusūdana's honesty — the Lord meets you where your faith actually is — means that beginning with a limited object of devotion is not failure; it is the starting point the grace is working through.

self_assessment: All six bhāṣyas present; renderings anchored in supplied Sanskrit prose with genuine doctrinal divergence; Śrīdhara payload was clean Sanskrit — no HTML artifacts detected; key tension (Madhusūdana's limiting clause 'na tu mad-viṣayāṃ śraddhāṃ') surfaced explicitly as the sharpest intra-panel divergence.
